coredata.dc:title: Dual ion specific electrochemical sensor using aminothiazole-engineered carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Keerthana, P.
George, Ashlay
Bharath, M.
Ghosh, Munmun
Varghese, Anitha
coredata.dc:description: A novel electrochemical sensor capable of concurrently detecting Pb2+ and Hg2+ ions has been innovatively engineered. This sensor utilizes the anodic stripping voltammetry technique (ASV) with a composite consisting of carbon quantum dots and aminothiazole (CQD-AT). In this composite, both the carbon quantum dots and aminothiazole contribute significantly to the electroactive surface area, boasting an abundance of functional groups that include oxygen and nitrogen atoms. These functional groups serve as active sites that enhance sensor sensitivity by facilitating the electrostatic interaction-based adsorption of heavy metal ions. Aminothiazole surface is evenly covered with CQDs, which are essential for metal gets reoxidized into metal ions for stripping analysis. Due to this unique modification, the Pb2+ and Hg2+ electrochemical sensor using the CQD-AT composite coated on carbon fiber paper electrode (CQD-AT/CFP) exhibits superior analysis performance such as wide linear range (0.6 × 10–11–160 × 10–6 M) for Pb2+ and Hg2+ with a limit of detection (LOD) of 3.0 pM and 6.2 pM for Pb2+ and Hg2+. CQD-AT/CFP modified electrode can be considered as a potential material for electrochemical simultaneous determination of Pb2+ and Hg2+ in different water samples.

head.ce:title: Dual ion specific electrochemical sensor using aminothiazole-engineered carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bangalore, Karnataka 560029, India

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Ashoka University, Haryana 131029, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel electrochemical sensor capable of concurrently detecting Pb2+ and Hg2+ ions has been innovatively engineered. This sensor utilizes the anodic stripping voltammetry technique (ASV) with a composite consisting of carbon quantum dots and aminothiazole (CQD-AT). In this composite, both the carbon quantum dots and aminothiazole contribute significantly to the electroactive surface area, boasting an abundance of functional groups that include oxygen and nitrogen atoms. These functional groups serve as active sites that enhance sensor sensitivity by facilitating the electrostatic interaction-based adsorption of heavy metal ions. Aminothiazole surface is evenly covered with CQDs, which are essential for metal gets reoxidized into metal ions for stripping analysis. Due to this unique modification, the Pb2+ and Hg2+ electrochemical sensor using the CQD-AT composite coated on carbon fiber paper electrode (CQD-AT/CFP) exhibits superior analysis performance such as wide linear range (0.6 × 10–11–160 × 10–6 M) for Pb2+ and Hg2+ with a limit of detection (LOD) of 3.0 pM and 6.2 pM for Pb2+ and Hg2+. CQD-AT/CFP modified electrode can be considered as a potential material for electrochemical simultaneous determination of Pb2+ and Hg2+ in different water samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid pace of industrialization has been a significant contributor to the escalation of heavy metal pollution in our environment. As industries expand and intensify, the release of toxic heavy metals into the air, water, and soil has become a pressing environmental concern. Vast industrial advancements and urbanization led to emission of toxic heavy metal ions including cadmium (Cd), arsenic (As), silver (Ag), chromium (Cr), lead (Pb), and mercury (Hg) [1] in to environment, which has high potential to cause serious threat to environment and human health even at ultra-trace amounts. These metals are persistent and can accumulate in ecosystems and living organisms, leading to a wide range of detrimental effects. In human beings the accumulation of heavy metals causes serious damage to vital organs like kidney, liver and further. Notably, lead and mercury exhibit various forms, encompassing metallic, organic, and inorganic states, with a prevalent +2 oxidation state [2,3]. Recent reports have specifically highlighted the serious cancer risks associated with the accumulation of Hg2+ and Pb2+ ions [4]. Lead finds extensive application across various industries, encompassing automotive, paint, battery manufacturing, ceramics, and plastics, primarily due to its unparalleled qualities such as exceptional ductility, softness, resistance to corrosion, and a low melting point. Meanwhile, the primary sources of mercury contamination in the environment stem from a combination of human-driven actions, such as excessive fossil fuel consumption, mining activities, and waste incineration, along with natural events like volcanic eruptions [5,6]. Specifically, human activities are the predominant factors responsible for the buildup of heavy metals in both the atmosphere and biological systems [4,7,8]. However, even minimal exposure to these substances can result in adverse health effects in humans, including kidney failure, autoimmune disorders, cognitive challenges, high blood pressure, liver impairment, anxiety, hallucinations, loss of consciousness, tremors, severe illnesses such as cancer, and even fatality [8–12].

Subsequent to the era of industrialization, the swift expansion of urban areas has led to the pollution of water and soil sources with Pb(II) and Hg(II). Consequently, these detrimental pollutants have infiltrated the food chain. European Community legislation has established strict guidelines, stipulating that the allowable concentrations of Pb2+ and Hg2+ in food should not surpass 0.2 and 0.5 µg/g (fresh weight) respectively [13,14]. Given this situation, there has been a growing urgency to monitor heavy metal ions in the environment, highlighting the need for the creation of analytical techniques capable of simultaneously detecting them. Simultaneous detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+ cations has been reported using various analytical methods, including atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [15], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [16], photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) [17], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [18], inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) [19], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [20] and photoelectrochemical sensor [21,22]. The methods rely on complex equipment, deal with high operational expenses, and involve laborious procedures. Consequently, there has been a growing need to create cost-effective, portable, and highly sensitive sensing tools, such as electrochemical sensors [23]. These sensors are considered exceptional alternatives to the aforementioned techniques due to their straightforward and affordable instrumentation, rapid analytical processes, suitability for colored and opaque samples, adaptability to various convenient forms, wide dynamic range, and enhanced accuracy [24,25]. The utilization of the square wave voltammetry technique enables the quantification of nano trace-level analytes with improved selectivity and heightened sensitivity [26]. This method involves the pre-concentration of analytes on the surface of the working electrode, with their subsequent stripping providing current responses corresponding to the concentration of the analyte [27].

An essential element in the construction of electrochemical sensors is the electrode material. The greater the surface area of the electrode, catalytic activity, electron transfer rate, and electrical conductivity are pronounced. Currently, nanoscale carbon materials, including carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanofibers (CNFs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and fullerenes (C60), are recognized as promising candidates for electrode materials [28,29]. A recent report has shown glassy carbon electrode modified with electrochemically reduced graphene oxide in bivalve mollusks acting as a sensor for Cd (II) and Pb (II) simultaneous detection [30]. Sarvestani et al. synthesized melamine-based Covalent organic frameworks for simultaneous detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+ with a linear range of 0.01–0.3 μmol/L and LOD was found to be as low as 0.00072 and 0.01211 μmol/L [31]. Nevertheless, when considering carbon-based materials, carbon quantum dots and their composites emerge as the most commonly employed options for the analysis of metal ions. This preference can be attributed to their unique structure and properties, with an emphasis on their excellent electrical conductivity.

Carbon quantum dots made from biomass frequently feature different surface functional (carboxyl, hydroxyl, nitro and epoxide) groups along their limits that might operate as reaction sites [32,33]. Additionally, many organic/inorganic moieties and biomolecules (such as antibodies, drugs, proteins, DNA, enzymes, and antigens etc.) may be immobilized on the surface of these functional groups. Furthermore, conjugated organic molecules and materials with charged groups like 2-aminothiazole (AT) could be anchored on their surface of functional groups using N-(3-(dimethyl aminopropyl)-N'-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) because of π-π conjugation and electrostatic adsorption. By doing so there was an improvement in the sensitivity for the sensor in electrochemical detection. Their additional advantage include biocompatibility [34], strong ability to transfer electrons, high surface area, and good characteristics materials for sensors and biosensors to screen physiological methods [35]. Lately, Shi et al. reported N-(2-aminoethyl)-N,N′,N′-tris-(pyridine-2-yl-methyl)ethane-1,2-diamine (AETPEA) functionalized carbon dot for improved Cu2+ detection with better selectivity over other metal ions. They have successfully utilized EDC/NHS chemistry to conjugate AETPEA to –COOH and –OH group in functionalized CQDs [36].

Given this context, developing more accurate analytical methods for identifying Pb2+ and Hg2+ ions by utilizing carbon quantum dots derived from biomass sources is intriguing. In this work, a CQD and 2-aminothiazole (AT) composite was synthesized employing EDC/NHS coupling and was subsequently coated on a bare CFP electrode for the simultaneous detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+ using SWASVs technique. CQDs and AT bring unique attributes to this composite sensor. Both components possess substantial electroactive surface areas and are rich in functional groups containing oxygen and nitrogen atoms. These functional groups serve as active sites for efficient electrostatic interactions, enhancing the adsorption of heavy metal ions and thereby increasing the sensor's sensitivity. Considering that combining various carbon materials on the carbon fiber paper (CFP) electrode, has the potential to enhance the electrocatalytic properties of the system by increasing the available surface area and thereby facilitating the transfer of analytes to the bulk solution, we aim to demonstrate the effectiveness of the modified electrode in detecting heavy metal ions Pb2+ and Hg2+. The detection limits for both Pb2+ and Hg2+ ions were found to be 3.0 pM and 6.2 pM which is under the safety limit defined by European Community legislation and WHO. Modified electrode was also used for the detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+ ions in real water samples and were able to record satisfactory results, suggesting the modified electrode can be considered as a promising candidate for the simultaneous sensing of Pb2+ and Hg2+ ions.

Celosia cristata (cockscomb) flower was taken from a local garden (Tamil Nadu, India). The following metal chlorides were purchased from Sigma Aldrich India: Cd2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Al2+, Fe2+, Cr2+, Ni2+ and Zn2+. Ethanol and cadmium metal carbonates were procured from Qualigens., India. Pb(NO3)2 was purchased from SD fine Chem India. Sulfuric acid, K3[Fe(CN)6], K2HPO4, K4[Fe(CN)6], CH3COOH, boric acid, and phosphoric acid were procured from Sigma Aldrich-Merck. Double-distilled (DD) water was used to make all the aqueous solutions. Carbon Fiber Paper (CFP) was bought from Toray Industries Inc.

The surface characteristics of the modified electrode and the quantum dots were determined using the Fourier-Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectrum, which was collected using Thermo Nicolet, Avatar. JEOL (JEM-F200) electron magnification microscope at a 200 kV voltage was used to take TEM pictures. To record XRD peaks, the Rigaku Smart Lab X-ray Diffractometer Cu K radiation (=1.5406) were used. To detect the peak range with a 532 nm laser, a lab RAM HR FT-Raman module was used. X-beam photoelectron spectrometer ESCA-14 (Omicron Nanotechnology) was used to record the XPS range. The UV–Vis 1800 spectrophotometer from Shimadzu was used to measure the absorption wavelength. The Shimadzu spectrofluorometer was used to measure fluorescence (PL) (excitation and emission) (RF5301PC).

Electrochemical analyzer model CHI609 was used to record the electrochemical measurements, which were obtained using the square wave anodic stripping voltammetry (SWASV) and Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) techniques (CH Instruments, Inc. USA). A traditional three-electrode arrangement was used for the electrochemical work. Modified CFP-working electrode, Platinum (Pt)-counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl-reference electrode respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (CQD)
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were freshly made from Celosia cristata flower using a green, one-step process, as shown in Scheme 1 . To get rid of the contaminants on the surface of the flower, the flowers were collected and cleansed repeatedly with double-distilled water. Which were then ground into powder followed by exposure to sun for two days. 5 g of the grounded powder was dissolved with 100 mL of water and the solution was extracted. The extracted solution was filtered off and poured into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated to 200 °C for 14 h. The autoclave was allowed for natural cooling to ambient temperature once the reaction was finished. To remove the unreacted big particles, a brown concentrated solution was centrifuged at 35,000 rpm for 30 min. A syringe filter (0.22 μM membrane) was used to filter the resulting solution. Unreacted products from fractional carbonization were removed by dialysis for 48 h. For further studies, the obtained solution was kept in refrigerator at 4 °C (Tables S1, S2).

An EDC/NHS coupling process was used to conjugate CQDs to 2-aminothiazole. Carboxyl groups on the surface of the CQDs were activated by dissolving 0.5 g of CQDs in 20 mL of 100 % ethanol. pH of the solution was then adjusted to 6.8 prior to addition of 0.38 g of EDC. Resultant solution was agitated for 30 min at room temperature, followed by addition of 0.3 g of AT with 0.12 g of NHS and stirred for 48 h at 50 rpm. Resulting solution were poured into a centrifuge tube and centrifuged for 25 min at 11000 rpm to remove the unreacted aminothiazole, EDC, and NHS. The final product was then stored at 4 °C for future studies. Table S3 explains the optimizing steps for the CQD-AT composite synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrode fabrication using CQD- aminothiazole composite and electrochemical measurement
ce:section.ce:para: CFP with dimensions 0.7 cm wide, 2.0 cm long, and 0.02 cm thickness was used for the electrochemical studies. The geometrical area of the working electrode was maintained as 0.7 cm2. For further electrical interaction, a copper wire was attached to the CFP, and polytetrafluoroethylene tape was used to cover beyond 1 cm of the exposed area. To make a slurry the CQD-AT composite, it was mixed with polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP) (8:1:1 ratio) and was directly applied on bare CFP electrode employing brush coating technique and cured overnight at 65 °C under vacuum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: Square wave anodic stripping voltammetry was utilized for the simultaneous detection of heavy metal ions. Before accumulating and reducing Hg2+ and Pb2+ metal ions, the CQD-AT/CFP electrode was immersed in Britton Robinson (BR) buffer solution containing metal ions under the conditions of a −1.2 V deposition potential and deposition duration of 150 s. Then, using anodic stripping voltammetry, the metals were stripped at potentials between −1.0 V and 1.0 V with a 4 mV step potential, 25 mV amplitude, 15 Hz frequency as shown in Scheme 2 . For the next measurement, the modified electrode was cleaned using potential −0.2 V for 80 s.

The developed method has been validated by analysing Pb2+ and Hg2+ ions in various water samples, such as lake, river and tap water samples, under precisely controlled parameters. Whatman filter paper was used to filter all the water samples that were collected. Water samples were allowed for two to three days to settle while being stored at 4 °C for further use. An aliquot of the solution was analysed for the determination of Pb2+ and Hg2+ ions by using the developed method. Concentration of the ions were determined by using the calibration graphs constructed. The recovery ability of the CQD-AT/CFP sensor was demonstrated by analysing the samples of water from different sources contaminated with Pb2+ and Hg2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQD and CQD-AT composite
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the successful synthesis of the CQD, and the composite, we conducted a comprehensive characterization of the samples using XRD, Raman for CQD and TEM, FTIR, and XPS for the composite. The XRD pattern for CQD explained in Fig. S1a which reveals a broad peak at around 2ϴ = 22.40°, correlates to (002) plane and CQD interlayerspace distance was found to be 0.39 nm. The peaks demonstrate high amorphousness of the CQD with the sp3 domain. Fig. S1b explains the Raman spectra of CQD, the D band positioned at 1354.61 cm−1, and G band positioned at 1584.22 cm−1. The G band shows the C-C atoms graphitic peak in a 2D lattice, while the D band represents a distorted carbon atom. The synthesized CQD is highly amorphous, as perceived by the observed ID/IG ratio of 1.54. The size and morphology of the CQD and CQD-AT composite were assessed via TEM and are presented in Fig. 1 .

It is clear from Fig. 1(a–h) that the TEM images of the as-synthesized CQD and CQD-AT exhibit a quasi-spherical form. According to Fig. 1 (a and b) the CQDs exhibited a size distribution ranging from 2 to 6 nm, with an average size of 3.75 nm, Fig. 1c explains the SAED pattern of CQD shows the diffused concentric rings suggesting the presence of amorphous carbon in the planes (002), (101). According to Fig. 1 (d and e), the size distribution of CQD-AT is controlled in the 2.5–6 nm range. Fig. 1 f explains the SAED pattern of CQD-AT shown additional rings which indicated the poly-crystalline nature of the composite present at (002), (004), (101), (200). The CQD-AT average diameter was found to be 4.25 nm. The average particle size distribution of CQD and CQD-AT for more than 100 nanoparticles is shown in the histogram in Fig. 1g and h [37,38].

In the IR spectra of CQD-AT composite, the O-H and N-H absorption bands for CQD and AT can be observed at 3407 cm−1 as a broad spectrum. The respective C-N vibrations bands can be seen between 1384 and 1106 cm−1 and the peaks at 2933 and 2853 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibrations of CH3 and CH2 [39]. The sharp band at 1672 cm−1 is linked with the carbonyl stretching vibration CO, whereas the bands at 1061 cm−1 are combined with the C-O band stretching vibration [40]. The weaker band at 1592 cm−1 indicates that the carbonyl (CO) band and stretching vibration (CC) band are combined [40,41]. Absorption bands were positioned at 709 and 682 cm−1 corresponds to the C-S stretching vibration. Taken all together, above results suggest the formation of CQD-AT composite as shown in Fig. S1c.

Further to prove the formation of CQD-AT, XPS analysis was performed (Fig. 2 ). As revealed from Fig. 2a, the survey spectra of CQD-AT/CFP showed four C1s, N1s, O1s, and S2p distinct peaks with 285, 400, 531.4, and 165.73 eV binding energies respectively. The CQD-AT/CFP primary core-level spectrum areas are shown in Fig. 2(b–e). Components C, O, N, and S were represented by percentages of 75.6 %, 20.1 %, 2.1 %, and 1.8 %, respectively. The C1s core level XPS spectrum is displayed in Fig. 2b and contains C-C/CC large intensity peak positioned at 284.80 eV [42–44].The carbonyl group (C-O bond) makes an appearance at 286.90 eV. The C- N less intense bond possesses a binding energy of 285.99 eV whereas the other indication of amine group CN bond possess a binding energy of 285.59 eV. The peak was observed to be less intense at 286.6 eV which is indicative of C-S. Another indication for the carbonyl group presence (CO) was confirmed by a strong peak at 289.95 eV in the finally modifiedelectrode. The three distinct N1s core-level spectra as shown in Fig. 2c, represents -NH, C-N and CN bonds. The N-H bond peak positioned at 400.9 eV, and the highly intense peak (C-N) is positioned at 399.8 eV. The less intense CN peak was observed at 401.5 eV. In Fig. 2d, the high-resolution peak for O1s displays four deconvoluted peaks with binding energies of 531.42, 532.50, 533.2, and 534.6 eV. The CQDs made from synthetic biomass showed a high peak at 531.42 eV because of the carbonyl group on their surface. The C-O bond positioned at 532.50 eV due to the surface carboxyl group of CQD-AT/CFP. The peak at 533.59 eV is attributed to hydroxyl (-OH) or carbonyl group (C-O-C/C-O-H) deposited on CQD-AT/CFP electrode. The carbonyl moieties on the surface of the CQD-AT cause the COO– bond to existing at 534.6 eV. The S 2p high-resolution core-level spectra confirm that the single sulphur (S) gets combined with CQD (Fig. 2e). Two doublets that occur at 163.32 eV and 164.3 eV, respectively, provide evidence in favor of this. The detectable asymmetric tail brought about by the doping technique, in which the thiophene-delocalized electrons raise the binding energy (eV) of the S atom at high binding energy levels. Therefore, the data mentioned above proved that CQD-AT had been deposited on the CFP electrode [45].

To prove the deposition of lead (Pb0) and mercury (Hg0) in the CQD-AT/CFP composite was confirmed through XPS analysis. As revealed from Fig. 3 a, the survey spectra of CQD-AT + Pb0 + Hg0/CFP showed six C1s, N1s, O1s, S2p, Pb0 and Hg0 distinct peaks with 285, 400, 531, 165, 140 and 99 eV binding energies respectively. The C1s core level XPS spectrum is displayed in Fig. 3b and contains C-C/CC large intensity peak positioned at 284.60 eV. The carbonyl group (C-O bond) makes an appearance at 286.90 eV. The C-N less intense bond possesses a binding energy of 286.6 eV whereas the other indication of amine group CN bond possess a binding energy of 285.46 eV. The peak was observed to be less intense at 286.2 eV which is indicative of C-S. Another indication for the carbonyl group presence (CO) was confirmed by a strong peak at 288.4 eV in the finally modifiedelectrode (CQD-AT + Pb0 + Hg0/CFP). The three distinct N1s core-level spectra as shown in Fig. 3c, represents –NH bond which is positioned at 400.5 eV, C-N at 399.8 eV and CN bond present at 401.5 eV respectively. In Fig. 3d, the high-resolution peak for O1s displays four deconvoluted peaks with binding energies of 531.4, 532.5, 533.2, and 534.7 eV corresponds to CO, C-O, C-O-C/C-O-H and COO– bond respectively. The S 2p high-resolution core-level spectra confirm that the single sulphur (S) gets combined with CQD (Fig. 3e). Two doublets that occur at 163.4 eV and 164.6 eV, respectively, provide evidence in favour of this [46]. The deposition of lead and mercury metal ion in the modified electrode then metal ion loses its electrons during stripping and become zero valence state (Pb2+ - Pb0, Hg2+ - Hg0) and its binding energy was confirmed using XPS analysis. The XPS spectra of Hg 4f 5/2 present in two different form, the binding energies present at 99.5 eV and 101.8 eV corresponds to zero state of mercury (Hg0) as shown in Fig. 3f [47]. When decomposing Pb4f into two peaks, the binding energy of 136.7 eV corresponds to Pb0 4f7/2 and Pb0 4f5/2 is present at 141.5 eV as shown in Fig. 3g [48].

Cyclic voltammetry was used to compare the performance of the applied surface modification of the bare/CFP, CQD/CFP, and CQD-AT/CFP electrodes. A typical probe made of a 5 mM K3[Fe (CN)6]/K4[Fe (CN)6] redox solution and a supporting electrolyte 0.1 M KCl was used to evaluate the electrochemical current response of all the modified electrodes. The potential range −0.4 to 0.8 V (E) vs SCE at the scan rate (50 mV s−1), the bare, CQD/CFP, AT/CFP and CQD-AT/CFP electrodes were all swept. As shown in Fig. 4 a, all of the modified and bare electrode surfaces generate distinctive and independent redox peaks.

Due to the CFP electrode's porous structure, the modified electrodes' conductivity and current value subsequently increased. The redox peak that was generated in bare CFP had a greater ΔV of 2.21 V. Due to the electrical conductivity of the huge carbon source of the quantum dots, a decrease in ΔV (1.48 V) with an increase in the CQDs catalytic activity on the CFP electrode. The AT shows ΔV (1.52 V) indicates the decreasing catalytic activity of the CFP electrode. The current density increased by four times after CQD-AT was coated on the CFP electrode. Thus, it reduces ΔV to 1.3 V while speeding up the transport of electrons to the modified electrode surface. The effective electrocatalytic activity of the CQD-AT/CFP electrode possess low ΔV with enhanced current density values. The concentration of the analyte (C), surface area of electrode–electrolyte (A) region in cm2, and diffusion coefficient (Dο) are identified from the calibration graph through Randle's-Sevcik equation (Eq. (2)).

Utilizing CV, the activity of CQD-AT /CFP electrodes with varying concentrations of CQD (0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, and 0.25 mg/mL) and AT (0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and 2.0 mg/mL) toward the redox probe was observed (Fig. S2a–b). As shown in Fig. 4a, compared to the bare, AT/CFP and CQD/CFP electrodes, the addition of AT on the CQD/CFP electrode increased the catalytic activity. The nanocomposite (CQD-AT) with the highest current response shown in 0.20 mg/mL of CQD and 1.0 mg/mL of AT. Therefore, 0.20 mg/mL of CQD and 1.0 mg/mL of AT were determined to be the ideal quantity and were used going forward to fabricate electrodes. Bare electrode, CQD/CFP electrode, AT/CFP and CQD-AT/CFP electrode electroactive surface area was found to be 0.703, 1.58, 1.75 and 2.97 cm2 correspondingly. The significant increase in final modified CQD-AT/CFP electrode, indicates higher electrocatalytic activity than the bare CFP and CQD-AT/CFP electrodes.

To investigate individual contributions to components' dielectric and electric properties, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out and the data was interpreted using Nyquist plot. This study demonstrates that the semicircle width of impedance is equivalent to electron charge transfer resistance (RCT) [49]. Various doses of CQD (0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, and 0.25 mg/mL) and AT (0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0 mg/mL) were used in the EIS measurement of CQD-AT/CFP electrodes (Fig. S2c–d). 0.20 mg/mL of CQD and 1.0 mg/mL of AT were chosen for the synthesis of nanocomposite (CQD-AT) and it shows lower RCT value and high charge transfer resistance. The RCT varied accordingly with respect to the available surface-active sites, we assume the random observation in RCT in our case could be due to the variance of available functionalities on CQD. As a result, it was decided that 0.20 mg/mL of CQD and 1.0 mg/mL of AT were the best concentrations to employ when fabricating electrodes. It was also used to discover more about the variations among the four CFP electrodes. The Nyquist plots for the bare/CFP, CQD/CFP, AT/CFP, and CQD-AT/CFP are shown in (Fig. 4b–c). Randel's equivalent circuit model was used to fit the signals to the data from the EIS measurements. The solution resistance (RS), double-layer capacitance (CDL), and electrode surface resistance to charge transfer (RCT) are all included in the electrical circuit model. The semi-circular diameter (RCT) values change as modifiers adsorb on the electrode's surface. An RCT value of around 1200 Ω was found for the bare electrode, showing that low conductivity and weak sensitivity are caused by high charge transfer resistance. Compared to the bare electrode, the charge transfer resistance (RCT) for the AT/CFP electrode, CQD/CFP electrode are lowered to 52 Ω and 48 Ω respectively. The final modified electrode (CQD-AT/CFP) exhibited a lower charge transfer resistance of 28 Ω and greater conductivity than other electrodes (Bare, CQD/CFP, AT/CFP) as a result of having carbon-rich quantum dots and aminothiazole applied to it.

Pb2+ and Hg2+ voltammetric response was studied using analytical methods to determine the effects of electrochemical and chemical factors (supporting electrolyte effect, effect of pH, accumulation potential, and accumulation time)[50].

Optimisation of supporting electrolytes by using voltammetric studies are shown in Fig. 5 (a) (formate, phosphate, and Britton-Robinson buffer). Owing to the potential of precipitate formation with the phosphate buffer (PBS) solution, the current performs poorly in phosphate buffer when compared to Britton-Robinson (BR) and formate buffers. Phosphate and formate buffers show no discernible variations in the Pb2+ and Hg2+ analyte current signal, whereas 0.1 mol/L of the BR buffer, pH 6 results show an increase in the current signal for Pb2+ and Hg2+. According to these findings, 0.1 mol/L (pH 6) BR buffer solution was selected for additional measurements. Entire working factors were enhancedusing a one-factor-at-a-time strategy to get the best sensitivity. The first investigated factor was the optimisation of various concentration of CQD and AT for the preparation of CQD-AT/CFP electrodes. Fig. S3(a–b) demonstrates the current response of cations during anodic stripping, which increased dramatically with increasing concentrations of CQD (0.05–0.25 mg/mL) and AT (0.5–2.5 mg/mL) in CQD-AT/CFP electrode. However, as CQD and AT concentrations increases higher, the peak currents for both the analytes were found to be decreased. In this regard, the optimal concentration of CQD-AT composite was chosen to be 0.20 mg/mL of CQD and 2.0 mg/mL of AT.

The influence of pH on the voltammetric current response for 20 µM of Pb2+ and Hg2+ at CQD-AT/CFP electrode was analysed using BR buffer ranging from pH 2–7. The highest current intensity was obtained at pH 6 (Fig. 5b). The current decline at lower pH values was attributed to the competition between analytes (Pb2+ and Hg2+) and protons in the medium of the modified electrode (CQD-AT/CFP) surface active sites. Further measurements were done using pH 6 solution of BR buffer.

The reduction potential of an analyte (Eacc) and the amount of time (tacc) needed for species to deposit or reduce on the modified electrode (CQD-AT/CFP) surface which controlled by the preconcentration stage of the SWASV process. Between −1.20 and 1.0 V, the Eacc impact on the 20 µM of Pb2+ and Hg2+ current signals were investigated using CQD-AT/CFP electrode (Fig. 5c). The significant current increases for both species at a deposition potential lesser than −1.0 V, and it reached a maximum potential of −1.20 V for Pb2+ and Hg2+. The evolution of H2 causes both species signal currents to drop at a potential more negative than −1.20 V, leading us to choose the best Eacc at this potential.

The association between the current signal and accumulation time (tacc) for the 20 µM of Pb2+ and Hg2+ utilizing CQD-AT/CFP is shown in Fig. 5d. As tacc rises, the analyte current signal increases for both species and reached its peak in 180 s. Then, a plateau, connected to an adsorption equilibrium between the analytes and the surface of the composite is observed. The analytical validation was developed with a tacc of 150 s to prevent quick saturation of the active CQD + AF/CFP sites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Individual and simultaneous detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+ ion
ce:section.ce:para: A comparative voltametric profile for 20 µM of Pb2+ and Hg2+ with bare CFP, CQD/CFP, AT/CFP and CQD-AT/CFP electrodes are shown in Fig. 6 a. Maximum current response 2.08 E-4 A for Pb2+ and 1.364E-4 A for Hg2+, respectively were observed with CQD-AT/CFP electrode. Using SWASV technique, Pb2+ and Hg2+ concentrations were optimally evaluated separately and concurrently by the modified electrode. Fig. 6b and c illustrates the Pb2+ peak current response at various concentrations (in individual mode). The concentration of Pb2+ were found in the range of 0.6 × 10–11–160 × 10–6 M, having linear relationship in both the range using the equation i/A = 1.04173E-4 (Pb2+) ± 3.6914 E-8. LOD for Pb2+ was calculated as 0.540 × 10–11 M in individual measurements. For Hg2+ a similar concentration range was taken of (0.6 × 10–11–160 × 10–6 M) and fitted with straight lines (i/A = 7.1188E-5 (Hg2+) ± 9.00492 E-8, correlation coefficient 0.998, 0.9898 respectively). The estimated LOD was found to be 0.340 × 10–11 M (Fig. 6d & e). The voltammograms produced by the SWASVs analysis for the simultaneous measurement of Pb2+ and Hg2+ at increasing concentrations using CQD-AT/CFP modified electrode are shown in Fig. 7 a.

The proposed modified electrode displayed well-divided, high-resolution peaks for metal ions (Pb2+ and Hg2+) at −0.528 V vs SCE for Pb2+ and −0.292 V vs SCE for Hg2+. In a simultaneous analysis, the current responses for Pb2+ shown two linear regions over the similar range 0.6 × 10–11–160 × 10–6 M (linearization equations i/A = 1.20837E–6 (Pb2+) ± 2.866 E-8 (0.6 × 10–11-–4.80 × 10–11), and 4.521E-8 (Pb2+) ± 6.25 E-8 (1 × 10–9–160 × 10–6 M) (Fig. 7b). The LOD values were found to be 3 pM, 0.2 nM respectively, given the correlation coefficients are 0.9980 & 0.9990 for Pb2+ions. Similarly, linear regions were obtained for Hg2+ in the concentration range 0.6 × 10–11–160 × 10–6 M using linear regression equations i/A = 3.232E-6(Hg2+) ± 4.114E-8 (0.6 × 10–11–5 × 10–11) and 5.7013 E-6 (Hg2+) ± 6.744 E-7 (1 × 10–9–160 × 10–6 M) (Fig. 7c). For Hg2+, the correlation coefficient and LOD values were found to be 0.9994, 0.984 and 1.54 nM, 6.2 pM respectively.

Table S4 shows the analytical parameters comparison (individual and simultaneous determination of Pb2+ and Hg2+ ion) of the modified sensor. The proposed method sensitivity has been significantly improved when both cations are analysed simultaneously. The results that were obtained are similar to the values that have been published in the literature [26,27,43–45]. Table 1 shows the comparison table of previously reported analytical methods for detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+ where this work shows a very low detection limit for both Pb2+ and Hg2+ with a wide linear range.

Selectivity towards the measurement of Pb2+ and Hg2+ was studied by adding 10-fold interfering ions, including Cd(II), Zn(II), Mn(II), Al(III), Zn(II), Ni(II), Fe(II) and Cr(II) into 0.1 M pH 6.0 BR buffer solution containing Pb2+ and Hg2+, CQD-AT/CFP. According to Fig. 8 , the stripping peak currents for Pb2+ and Hg2+ both shifts slightly with errors around ± 5 %, indicating a high CQD-AT/CFP anti-interference ability for the co-existence of the two heavy metal ions. The possible reasons for this can be (i) the production of intermetallic compound (ii) competition between the analytes and interfering ions for the active sites of the CQD-AT/CFP electrode. The existing standard addition approach was used for the simultaneous measurement, permitting the detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+ in complex matrices without any interference from associated molecules or ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Repeatability, reproducibility, and stability of the designed electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+ was monitored and measured on 5 different modified electrodes that were produced individually under same experimental conditions in order to assess the repeatability and reproducibility of the sensor (Fig. 9 a and b). Results revealed a relative standard deviation of 4.75 %, which points to the modified electrodes remarkable repeatability. With the help of 80 µM of Pb2+ and Hg2+, the produced sensor's accuracy was examined. The developed sensor maintained 94.5 % of the current response when tested for storage stability using the similar electrodes after it is exposed to air for 15 days, which is due to the binding properties of rich quantum dots and aminothiazole (CQD-AT) that appeared on the electrode's surface. Even after being used more than ten times, the sensor produced identical results. These findings consequently support the improved storage stability, repeatability, and reproducibility of the modified electrodes.

The plausible mechanism for the simultaneous detection of heavy metal ions is proposed and the detailed stepwise pathway is demonstrated in Scheme 2. Experimental results were evaluated primarily to confirm the activity of the modified AT/CQD electrode. From electrochemical studies and XPS analysis deposition of Pb and Hg atoms on the electrode surface was confirmed. Better activity of AT/CQD electrodes compared to CQD electrodes opened up the possibilities for multiple pathways in the adsorption mechanism of the heavy metal ions. The possible mechanism for adsorption can follow two major pathways i) electrostatic adsorption and ii) complexation adsorption [51]. In electrostatic adsorption pathway, there is a strong ion pair interaction between the modified electrode and heavy metal ion. In our case electrostatic adsorption of heavy metal ions onto modified electrode is undeniable as AT/CQD electrode offers larger surface area. The high surface area offered by the carbon matrix and the electron rich functionalities also resulted in easier electron transfer between the electrode and the heavy metal ions [32,52,53]. Apart from these the superior sensing property of AT/CQD may be due to complexation adsorption of heavy metal ions on to modified electrode [51,52].

CQD contains various groups like carboxylic acid, hydroxyl and amine as functionalities, incorporating aminothiazole rings onto CQD results in formation of additional appended groups on their surface. These freely suspended groups protruding out from the CQD surface contain electron rich atoms like amide nitrogen, ring nitrogen, ring sulphur and so on. These atoms can render lone pair of electrons to heavy metal ions resulting complexation adsorption. The adsorbed heavy metal ions could then be reduced to their zero-valence state resulting their accumulation on the electrode surface. During stripping process these zero valent atoms will be oxidized to their divalent state. Through this cyclic process of adsorption (electrostatic and complexation) and stripping heavy metal ions can be efficiently removed from water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Pb2+ and Hg2+ ion in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The CQD-AT/CFP were used as a modified electrode to determine the presence of Pb2+ and Hg2+ metal ions in real water samples (i.e., lake water, river water, and tap water) was analysed using Atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) (standard addition method) and electrochemical method as shown in Fig. S4. Table 2 delivers a summary of the determination outcomes for the spiked water. Simultaneous Pb2+ and Hg2+ measurement in real water samples show good recovery percentage in the range of 98–115 % with RSD less than 2 %, indicating the method's strong practical applicability.

In this work, a straightforward, quick, and highly sensitive electrochemical sensor for simultaneous detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+ ions were developed using a carbon quantum dot and aminothiazole (CQD-AT) composite. The concentration of CQD-AT composite pH, nature of supporting electrolyte, and electrochemical parameters all were initially tuned using one factor-at-a-time method. The fabricated electrochemical sensor had a good two straight linear current response for Pb2+ and Hg2+, over 0.6 × 10–11 –160 × 10–6 M concentration, with LOD 3 pM and 6.2 pM under optimal conditions. Testing the constructed sensors selectivity across a broad range of ionic species revealed that the electrode exhibits a permissible selectivity for both analytes. Thus, the developed sensor in this research work is better in terms of application, speed, and cost effectiveness. Finally, Pb2+ and Hg2+ were successfully detected in real water samples, demonstrating the CQD-AT on CFP electrode used for the study of trace heavy metal ions in practical applications.
